statement.markdown: Given a string of HTML tags, validate if they follow a strict nesting structure. The string will contain only uppercase letters, spaces, and '<' or '>' characters. Each '<' character represents the start of a new tag, and each '>' character represents the end of a tag. A tag is valid if it has a corresponding closing tag with the same name or is a self-closing tag (e.g., '<img/>'). The string starts and ends with a '<' character. The task is to write a function that returns true if the HTML string is valid, and false otherwise.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the HTML string is valid
statement.notes: The string may contain comments (<!-- -->), doctype declarations, and other HTML elements.

constraints.description: The number of tags in the input string

examples.explanation: The HTML string is valid as it follows a strict nesting structure
The HTML string is invalid as it contains a mismatched closing tag
The HTML string is valid as it follows a strict nesting structure

hints.text: Use a stack to keep track of opening tags
Iterate over the string and check for mismatched closing tags

editorial.title: Editorial: Fixing Invalid Nested HTML Structure
editorial.intuition: The problem requires a stack-based approach to validate the HTML string
editorial.bruteForce: A brute-force approach would involve parsing the string and manually checking for mismatched tags
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would involve using a stack to keep track of opening tags
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves using a stack to keep track of opening tags and iterating over the string to check for mismatched closing tags
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The solution is correct as it accurately checks for mismatched tags and handles self-closing tags
commonMistakes: Mistake 2: Not checking for mismatched closing tags
